feedback.text: Good day! First of all, I would like to celebrate this initiative and the good it will do for all consumers in the EU. I have long been concerned with the predatory marketing and UI design tactics many companies use, especially in games aimed at children, so seeing it adressed brings some peace to me. I would love to see this act go even further in protecting consumer rights, and I have a suggestion for what could be added alongside the already existing points. Currently, many companies in the videogame industry do not have any plans of keeping online games available to play past the end-of-life date. Some of those games could be plenty entertaining as singleplayer offline games, others could be playable on fan-run servers if the company provided server software. However, very often buyers of an online game find themselves unable to do anything once the company-run servers shut down, sometimes even having the game's license revoked, as happened with Ubisoft's game "The Crew". I have experienced a situation like this myself, and thus feel very strongly for every consumer that has bought a game (with the impression of buying a good, not a service), only for it to end up unplayable. My suggestion is for every company or developer to provide a clear end-of-life plan for their online games, and additionally be very clear, not only in the EULA, but also in the digital or physical gamestore, in what is sold: game or license. I am certain (based on the million or so signatures on the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative) that many consumers across the entire EU would gain even more benefit from the Digital Fairness Act this way. Thank you for your consideration, and this already greatly consumer-protective initiative. I hope the EU can continue to be a beacon of hope in these difficult times.
